{"success": true, "name": "nexa-paraguay-deployment", "description": "Build and deploy the Nexa Paraguay site to Docker Swarm on VPS 72.61.44.159", "tags": [], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: nexa-paraguay-deployment
description: Build and deploy the Nexa Paraguay site to Docker Swarm on VPS 72.61.44.159
---

# Nexa Paraguay Deployment

## ⚠️ CRITICAL UPDATE: May 11 2026 — Pricing & Model Overhaul

**The $2,900/$4,400/$6,900 4-tier model was WRONG.** It was based on market research estimates, not the client's actual business. After the May 11 meeting with Sonia (founder):

| Old Assumption | Reality |
|---------------|---------|
| Price: $2,900-$6,900 (4 tiers) | **$1,500 — single service** |
| Clients/month: 5-10 | **0.5-1** |
| Monthly costs: $10K-$18K | **~$0 fixed** (phone + fuel) |
| Business: 4-tier programs | **One service + add-ons + commissions** |
| Real revenue: residency fees | **Commissions** (properties 2.75%, vehicles, appliances) |

**Before any work on Nexa site content or pricing, read:**
- `docs/CHANGELOG.md` — every assumption that changed
- `docs/meetings/meeting-report-may-11.md` — full meeting outcome
- `docs/SITE_CHANGELOG.md` — all 25 changes needed on the site

**Do NOT rely on `pricing-matrix-complete.md` or `financial-model.md`** — they are deprecated with banners warning they contain wrong pricing.

## Current State (May 11 2026)

### Hybrid Dependencies
Sections, i18n, and client-kit stay on `.tgz` (the published `@ai-whisperers/sections@0.3.1` has a different API). All other `@ai-whisperers/*` come from GitHub Packages:
- `auth@0.2.0`, `admin@0.2.1`, `ui@0.4.0`, `seo@0.2.0`, `whatsapp@0.2.0`, `theme@0.2.0` — from npm
- `sections@0.1.0`, `i18n@0.2.0`, `client-kit@0.1.0` — from `.packages/*.tgz`

### Integrations Wired
- **HubSpot**: Contact form POSTs to `api.hsforms.com` with rate limiting + graceful fallback
- **Mailchimp**: `/api/subscribe` route with idempotent PUT + graceful degradation
- **GA4**: Measurement ID `G-XE49GLEP34` in env + DNS in CSP, gtag via Script in layout
- **Admin panel**: Dashboard at `/admin` with `AdminGuard` component (Supabase session check)
- **Content honesty**: Stats → `[Datos reales pendientes]`, testimonials → placeholders, TeamSection badges `⚠️ IA`

### Docker Build (current)
```bash
# From VPS
cd /root/nexa-paraguay
git pull origin main
docker build --no-cache -t nexa-paraguay:prod .
docker service update --force --image nexa-paraguay:prod nexa_web
```

## App Router Architecture (current)

- Framework: Next.js 16 App Router, React 19, TypeScript 5, Tailwind v4
- Routes use `[locale]/[slug]` pattern — 4 locales (es/en/nl/de), 25+ slug pages
- Content JSON-driven: `content/{locale}.json` (4 locales, `es.json` is source of truth at ~208KB), `nexa-pages/*.json` (26+ page configs), `images.json` (image registry)
- SectionsRenderer: `'use client'` component with 30+ sections loaded via `dynamic(() => import(...), { ssr: true })`
- PPR disabled (`cacheComponents` removed) — all routes are `● SSG` static HTML
- Each section component receives `pageContent` (the resolved content sub-object) and `data` (same, as fallback)
- Section content keys in page configs reference dot-paths into the locale JSON (e.g. `"home.hero"`)
- Dynamic routes using `generateStaticParams()` — all pages pre-rendered at build time
- Docker Swarm deployment with standalone output

### SectionsRenderer Content Resolution

**Critical rule for content keys in nexa-pages/*.json:**

Components in `sections.tsx` access `pageContent.KEY` (e.g., `HeroSection` does `pageContent.hero`).
Components in `FeedbackSection` use `data` directly (e.g., `data.title`).

| Component | What it reads | Correct content key |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------|
| HeroSection | `pageContent.hero` | `"home"` |
| TrustSection | `pageContent.trust` | `"home"` |
| ServicesSection | `d.services?.groups \|\| d.groups` | `"home"` |
| WhyCountrySection | `pageContent.whyCountry` | `"home"` |
| ProcessSection | `pageContent.process` | `"home"` |
| FeedbackSection | `data.title`, `data.eyebrow` | `"home.feedback"` |
| CtaBanner | `pageContent.finalCta \|\| pageContent.cta` | `"home"` |

If a section returns null, check the content key depth against what the component reads.

## Build & Deploy (current)

```bash
# Full clean rebuild (REQUIRED for SSG changes)
cd /root/nexa-paraguay
git pull origin main
docker build --no-cache -t nexa-paraguay:prod .
docker service update --force --image nexa-paraguay:prod nexa_web
sleep 5
curl -sL -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://nexa.paragu-ai.com
curl -sL -o /dev/null -w " admin:%{http_code}" https://nexa.paragu-ai.com/admin
echo ""
```

**CRITICAL — Always `docker build --no-cache`.** Without `--no-cache`, Docker may reuse the builder stage layer which contains the old `.next/` output even if the source files have been updated.

## Integration: HubSpot Contact Form

The contact form at `/es/contacto` POSTs to `/api/contact`, which:
1. Rate-limits by IP (10 requests/hour)
2. Sends fields (name, email, phone, message, program) to HubSpot API
3. Falls back to console.log if HubSpot is unreachable
4. Returns `{ ok: true }` regardless (never exposes errors to user)

**To verify:**
```bash
curl -s -X POST https://nexa.paragu-ai.com/api/contact \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"Test","email":"[REDACTED_EMAIL]","message":"Test submission"}'
# Response: {"ok":true,"remaining":9,"hubspot":"submitted"} or "logged"
```

## Integration: Mailchimp Subscribe

The newsletter form POSTs to `/api/subscribe`, which:
1. Uses Mailchimp API v3 PUT (idempotent — safe to retry)
2. Creates subscriber with status `subscribed`
3. Gracefully handles "already subscribed" (returns ok)
4. Falls back to console.log on API failure

**Required env var:** `MAILCHIMP_AUDIENCE_ID` and `MAILCHIMP_API_KEY` in `.env.local`

## Admin Panel

Located at `/admin`. Self-contained `AdminGuard` component:
1. Creates Supabase client inline (no `@ai-whisperers/auth` dependency)
2. Checks session via `sb.auth.getSession()`
3. Redirects to `/` if no session
4. Shows stats cards for pages/content/blogs

**On first deploy**, you need a Supabase user with admin role to log in.

## SSG Cache Invalidation

Key insight from this session: **all pages produce `BAILOUT_TO_CLIENT_SIDE_RENDERING`** in their SSG HTML `<main>` tags. This is the normal behavior of a `'use client'` SectionsRenderer wrapping dynamic imports — content renders on the client via RSC hydration, not in static HTML. See the generic pitfall in `nextjs-docker-swarm` skill (pitfall #22) for the full explanation and the two fix paths (static imports vs accepting RSC serialization).

Next.js 16 SSG + Docker Swarm creates **four layers of caching** that must all be cleared:

### 1. Docker layer caching
The builder stage runs `npm run build` inside it. `--no-cache` forces Docker to re-run the builder.
```bash
docker build --no-cache -t nexa-paraguay:prod .
```

### 2. Cloudflare/edge CDN cache
```bash
curl -sL -H "Cache-Control: no-cache" https://nexa.paragu-ai.com/en/contacto
```

### 3. Browser cache
Use incognito or hard-refresh when testing.

### Verification inside container
```bash
docker exec $(docker ps -q -f name=nexa_web) cat /app/.next/server/app/en/contacto.html | grep "expected content"
```

## CRITICAL PITFALL: JSON Corruption

The `patch` tool and `sed` can corrupt large JSON files (content/es.json is 208KB, 900+ lines). A missing comma or broken key breaks the entire site (content: {} empty response — all pages render as header+footer only). **Always validate after every JSON edit:**

```bash
python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('content/es.json')); print('VALID')"
```

## .npmrc Token Management

The `.npmrc` for GitHub Packages must have a valid classic PAT (`ghp_*`). Fine-grained PATs (`github_pat_*`) do NOT work with `npm.pkg.github.com`.

**To update a stale token:**
```bash
gh auth token
# Copy the output
sed -i 's|ghp_.*|<paste token>|' .npmrc
```

**To check if the current token works:**
```bash
NPM_CONFIG_USERCONFIG=.npmrc npm view @ai-whisperers/auth version --registry=https://npm.pkg.github.com
```

## Locale Configuration Pattern

**File:** `src/lib/locales.ts` — single source of truth for locale constants.

```typescript
export const LOCALES = ['es', 'en', 'nl', 'de'] as const
export const DEFAULT_LOCALE = 'es'
export const LOCALE_COOKIE = 'NEXT_LOCALE'
export type Locale = (typeof LOCALES)[number]
export const LOCALE_FLAGS: Record<string, string> = {
  es: '/images/flags/es.svg',
  en: '/images/flags/en.svg',
  nl: '/images/flags/nl.svg',
  de: '/images/flags/de.svg',
}
```

**tsconfig.json must use `./src/*` path:**
```json
"paths": { "@/*": ["./src/*"] }
```

## Content Architecture: Homepage vs Dedicated Pages

**Critical rule: NO duplicated content between homepage and dedicated pages.**

Each piece of content has ONE owner:

| Content | Owner Page | Homepage Shows |
|---|---|---|
| Programs tiers | /programas | Summary headline + CTA link |
| Services groups | /servicios | Summary headline + CTA link |
| Why Paraguay pillars | /por-que-paraguay | Summary headline + CTA link |
| Process steps | /proceso | Summary headline + CTA link |
| Testimonials | /casos-de-exito | CTA link only |
| Hero, Stats, Trust, Tax Calc, Benelux | / (homepage) | Full content (unique) |

## Client Business Context

> **Business model fully documented in repo.** For current pricing, target segments, brand decisions, and site changes needed, see:
> - `docs/CHANGELOG.md` — every assumption changed after May 11
> - `docs/meetings/meeting-report-may-11.md` — pricing/brand/slogan confirmed
> - `docs/SITE_CHANGELOG.md` — all 25 changes to implement on site
> - `docs/SOURCE_OF_TRUTH.md` — master client profile (pre-May 11 base + outcome)
> - `docs/01-client/client-analysis-complete.md` — full client analysis

**Contact:** Sonia Weiss, Nexa Paraguay. WhatsApp channel `[REDACTED_PHONE]`.

## Living Upgrade Plan

- The full 75-idea upgrade plan for Nexa Paraguay lives at:
  **https://github.com/Ai-Whisperers/infrastructure/blob/main/nexa-paraguay-upgrade-plan.md**
- Client discovery meeting (May 11 2026): **`docs/meetings/meeting-report-may-11.md`** — contains confirmed pricing, target segments, differentiators, and action items
- **Site changelog (25 changes needed):** `docs/SITE_CHANGELOG.md` — every single change from client feedback, ordered by priority (P0-P6)
- **Pricing/assumption change log:** `docs/CHANGELOG.md` — every assumption invalidated by May 11 meeting
", "path": "nexa-paraguay-deployment/SKILL.md", "skill_dir": "/root/.hermes/skills/nexa-paraguay-deployment", "linked_files": {"references": ["references/monorepo-architecture.md", "references/docker-build-monorepo.md", "references/adding-new-section-component.md", "references/section-registration-pitfall.md", "references/content-data-pollution-fix.md"], "scripts": ["scripts/clean-json-pollution.py"]}, "usage_hint": "To view linked files, call skill_view(name, file_path) where file_path is e.g. 'references/api.md' or 'assets/config.yaml'", "required_environment_variables": [], "required_commands": [], "missing_required_environment_variables": [], "missing_credential_files": [], "missing_required_commands": [], "setup_needed": false, "setup_skipped": false, "readiness_status": "available"}

Now I have a full picture. The key learnings from this session:

1. **User preference confirmed**: Ivan explicitly corrected "analyze in deapth" + wants bulk ops. The session confirmed his pattern: "work on all this", "do it completely".
2. **Competitive analysis technique** emerged — deep browser research of competitor site, extracting pricing, product structure, content strategy, political positioning.
3. **Client proposal document format** — created a structured proposal with options A/B/C, recommendations, and checkboxes for Sonia to decide. This is a reusable format for client decision-making documents.
4. **Meeting prep document format** — walkable blocks with exact Spanish wording, expected answers, notes fields.

The existing `client-feedback-pipeline` skill covers parts of this but the competitive analysis technique and the proposal document format are new patterns worth capturing.

The skill `client-content-audit` covers business intelligence extraction. The skill `client-feedback-pipeline` covers feedback processing. Neither covers **competitive analysis + proposal generation** as a combined workflow.

Let me create a reference under the `client-feedback-pipeline` umbrella for the proposal document format, and add the competitive analysis approach.

{"action": "write_file", "name": "client-feedback-pipeline", "file_path": "references/client-proposal-document-format.md", "file_content": "# Client Proposal Document Format

For presenting multiple options to a client (Sonia or any professional-services client) for them to choose and validate.

## When to Use

- After initial discovery/research, before building
- Client needs to see options, not just one recommendation
- Several unknowns remain that only the client can resolve
- Client has told us data that invalidated our assumptions, and we need to rebuild the plan

## Structure

### Header

```markdown
# [Client Name] — Propuesta Completa

> **Propósito:** [one-sentence purpose]
> **Documento vivo** — lo actualizamos después de cada reunión.
> **Formato:** Opciones con su contexto, costo, esfuerzo y recomendación.
```

### Each Proposal Section

Every decision point uses this structure:

```markdown
### X.X — [Decision Name]

**Contexto:** [Why this decision matters. 2-3 sentences.]

| Opción | Descripción | Ejemplo |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| **A** | Option A description | Concrete example |
| **B** | Option B description | Concrete example |
| **C** | Option C description | Concrete example |

**Recomendación:** ✅ Which option and why.

**Sonia decide:** ☐ A ☐ B ☐ C
```

### Additional Metadata Per Section (optional, when context is complex)

```
| Campo | Qué significa |
|-------|--------------|
| **Esfuerzo** | Hours to implement |
| **Impacto** | Bajo / Medio / Alto |
| **Riesgo** | What could go wrong |
| **Dependencia** | What we need from client to start |
```

### Document Sections (Typical Order)

1. **Pricing** — base price, tiers/add-ons, commissions, kids, quotes
2. **Site Structure** — pages to keep/delete/create, navigation, lead magnets
3. **Content** — voice, slogan, personal story borrador
4. **New Features** — calculators, maps, bots
5. **Competitor Analysis** — what to steal, adapt, avoid
6. **Photos & Assets** — checklist of what we need from client, with urgency
7. **Action Plan** — 3 phases: immediate (no dependency), post-meeting, ongoing
8. **Meeting Checklist** — questions to resolve in the meeting, sorted 🔴🟡🟢
9. **Client's Ideas** — blank space for them to add their own
10. **Approval** — signature block with date

### Language

- Always write in the **client's language** (Spanish for Paraguayan clients)
- Use "vos" form for Sonia (Paraguayan Spanish)
- Each question must have the EXACT wording the client will hear
- Expected answers help prepare counter-questions
- Notes fields are critical — you will NOT remember what they said 30 min into the conversation

### Key Behaviors

- Don't present only one option — clients want to choose
- Don't ask the same question twice — Appendix A exists for already-answered questions
- Don't send the full document as a cold message — walk through it together in a meeting
- After the meeting, the client marks what they approved with checkboxes
- Update the document after the meeting with their decisions
- Commit to the repo as the new source of truth
"}

{"action": "write_file", "file_content": "# Deep Competitor Analysis Workflow

For researching a competitor's full site structure, extracting pricing, product strategy, content approach, and producing actionable recommendations.

## When to Use

- Client names a specific competitor they're worried about
- You discover a strong competitor during market research
- Need to understand WHY a competitor is successful before positioning against them
- Client wants to borrow (not copy) elements from another site

## Step 1: Full Site Reconnaissance

Use `browser_navigate` to visit every major page of the competitor's site:

| Page Type | What to Extract |
|-----------|----------------|
| Home | Slogan, hero message, main CTA, featured content |
| Services/Products | All offerings with prices, what's included per tier |
| Pricing | Visible prices, payment options, discounts, currency |
| About/Team | Who are the key people, their backgrounds, credibility |
| FAQ | Objections they anticipate, their answers, tone |
| Blog/Content | Post frequency, topics, content mix (% politics, % practical, % community) |
| Contact | Lead capture mechanism, WhatsApp vs form vs email |
| Lead magnets | Ebooks, checklists, calculators — what captures emails |
| Expeditions/Events | Tours, packages, itineraries, prices |
| Legal pages | Compliance, delivery policy, refund policy |

## Step 2: Deep Page Content Extraction

For key pages (pricing, services, expeditions), extract the FULL content with `web_extract` or `web_extract_plus`. Don't rely on the snapshot alone — snapshots skip detailed text blocks.

Look for:
- **Exact prices** (including currency)
- **What's included vs what's extra**
- **Testimonial content** (who they quote, what clients say)
- **FAQ structure** (how they frame objections)
- **CTA phrasing** (urgency, risk-reversal, guarantees)

## Step 3: Content Strategy Analysis

Categorize the competitor's blog/content into buckets:

| Category | % of Posts | Example Topics |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| Tax/legal updates | ~30% | Regulatory changes, exit taxes |
| Political/freedom content | ~25% | Ideological topics, controversial figures |
| Community events | ~20% | Group tours, gatherings, aftermovies |
| Practical advice | ~15% | How-to guides, requirements, costs |
| Personal stories | ~10% | Founder experiences, client testimonials |

## Step 4: Build the Comparison Table

| Dimension | Competitor | Our Client |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Language | Dutch only | Spanish + 4 locales |
| Price | €2,800 (~$3,100) | **$1,500** |
| Target | Freedom-seekers, investors | Families, retirees |
| Tone | Political, fear-based | Personal, warm, "relax" |
| Location | Rural (Hohenau) | Urban (Asunción) |
| Property focus | Dedicated agent (Century 21) | Commission-based (free to client) |
| Group tours | ✅ Expeditions (€3,397-€5,397) | ❌ |
| Post-residency | Limited | ✅ Strong ($200/day, up to 1 year) |
| Blog | Active, political + tax | 30 posts (generic AI, needs overhaul) |
| Lead magnets | ✅ Multiple ebooks | ❌ Not yet |
| Bot | Telegram group (manual) | ✅ WhatsApp bot (coming) |
| Location data | None | ✅ 1,485 places with GPS |
| Overhead | High (expeditions, marketing) | Near zero |

## Step 5: Identify What to STEAL

Things to copy directly (they're generic best practices):

| Tactic | How to Adapt |
|--------|-------------|
| FAQ page | Compile client's answered questions into one page |
| Email capture on every page | Footer + blog + lead magnets + 404 page |
| Lead magnets | "10 cosas que nadie te contó sobre Paraguay" |
| Testimonials slider | Rotating testimonials on services page |
| Transparent pricing | Show price directly |
| 404 page with capture | Redirect errors to ebook download |

## Step 6: Identify What to ADAPT

Things to take and modify for the client's positioning:

| Competitor's Version | Client's Version |
|---------------------|-----------------|
| "Plan B" (fear/escape) | "Un nuevo capítulo" (positive/aspirational) |
| Legalistic blog post | Simple, personal, visual timeline |
| Residency timing calculator | Tax calculator |
| "Why Paraguay" (freedom) | "Why Paraguay" (quality of life, family) |

## Step 7: Identify What to AVOID

Things that DON'T fit the client's brand:

| Don't Copy | Why |
|-----------|-----|
| Political/fear-based messaging | Client is apolitical |
| Paid group expeditions | Too complex for client's bandwidth |
| YouTube channel | Client doesn't want to be a content creator |
| Crypto payments | Not needed at this volume |
| Container shipping | Client's clients come with luggage, not households |
| Telegram group | WhatsApp is the client's platform |

## Step 8: Identify the Client's ADVANTAGES

Things the competitor CANNOT easily copy:

| Advantage | Why It Matters |
|-----------|---------------|
| Price (47% cheaper) | Massive competitive gap |
| Personal story + reinvention | More relatable than political commentary |
| Family focus | Different target segment |
| Post-residency support | Competitor offers one-time service |
| Commission-free property help | "Free to client, paid by seller" |
| Neighborhood intelligence | 1,485 GPS places — no competitor has this |
| Local (not expat) | Understands both worlds |
| Lower overhead | Near zero costs = can underprice |

## Output Document Structure

```markdown
# [Competitor Name] — Competitive Analysis & Learnings

> **URL:** https://...
> **Competitor to:** [Client Name]

## 1. WHO THEY ARE
## 2. PRICING & PRODUCT STRUCTURE
## 3. SITE STRUCTURE & PAGES
## 4. CONTENT STRATEGY
## 5. WHAT WE CAN STEAL (Ethically)
## 6. WHAT TO ADAPT
## 7. WHAT NOT TO COPY
## 8. OUR ADVANTAGES
## 9. RECOMMENDED ACTIONS (Steal / Adapt / Avoid)
## 10. DIRECT COMPARISON TABLE
```
", "file_path": "references/competitor-analysis-workflow.md", "name": "client-feedback-pipeline"}

{"success": true, "name": "client-feedback-pipeline", "file": "SKILL.md", "content": "--- name: client-feedback-pipeline description: Process, prioritize, and implement client feedback on a live website. Covers feedback capture, analysis, change implementation across locales, ethical content decisions (removing fabricated data), and roadmap updates. triggers: - "client feedback" - "process feedback" - "implement changes from client" - "website revisions" - "client requested changes" - "update site from feedback" - "remove fake data" - "ethical content audit" --- # Client Feedback Pipeline Goal: Take raw client feedback (WhatsApp voice notes, texts, emails) and turn it into a clean, prioritized set of changes applied across all locales. ## Phase 1: Capture Save the raw feedback verbatim. The client's exact words matter for: - Distinguishing what they actually said vs what you inferred - Understanding their emotional framing ("that's bullshit", "I don't want to put something false") - Preserving approval-ready copy they already wrote **Save to a file** in the project under `docs/client-feedback-YYYY-MM-DD.md`. When the client writes section-by-section copy (e.g. "Our Services: The section is now clean..."), save that too — it's approved text you'll use directly. ## Phase 2: Analyze — Sort into 4 Buckets | Bucket | Signal | Action | |--------|--------|--------| | **Delete** | "remove", "quit", "doesn't need to be there", "unnecessary" | Remove the section/content immediately | | **Simplify** | "too much", "overcomplicated", "unclear" | Reduce to minimum viable version | | **Rewrite** | "I want it like this", client-provided copy | Replace with client's exact words | | **Ethical red flag** | "that's bullshit", "not real", "false", "fabricated" | Remove immediately, no negotiation | **Key insight:** When a client says fabricated data is "bullshit", don't argue or replace it with different data. Remove it. An empty section is honest; a fake one is a liability. ## Phase 3: Prioritize 1. **Ethical red flags** — first, always. These are legal/trust issues. 2. **Navigation/structure** — affects every page view 3. **Homepage hero** — highest visibility 4. **Content sections** — body of pages 5. **About page** — brand story 6. **Footer/SEO/meta** — lingering details ## Phase 4: Implement (Multi-Locale) ### JSON Field Verification: Data Added ≠ Data Rendered After adding data to JSON, **always verify the component actually renders it via a browser check.** Adding a new key like `resultsParagraphs` to the content JSON does NOT mean the page will display it — the React component must explicitly read and render that field. **Workflow for new-content rendering verification:** 1. Add data to all 4 locale JSON files 2. Update the component (source file in node_modules or monorepo) to read and render the new field 3. If the component is from a monorepo package: update dist/ AND copy the change upstream so it persists across npm install 4. Full clean build (rm -rf .next + docker build --no-cache) 5. Deploy with --force 6. Navigate to the page in a browser and visually confirm the content appears 7. If it doesn't appear: check that the component doesn't have an early return that skips the new content (`if (!d.title && !paragraphs.length) return null` may skip rendering even though other fields exist) **Do NOT rely on curl or grep to verify content rendering when PPR is active** — use browser screenshots or the snapshot tool. ### Turbopack src/ Resolution Trap (Monorepo Packages) **Turbopack may resolve `@scope/sections` from `packages/@scope/sections/src/` rather than `dist/`, even when `package.json` exports point to `./dist/index.js`.** This means: copying fresh dist to `node_modules/@scope/sections/dist/` is **not sufficient** when the site has a `packages/@scope/sections/` directory in the build context. Turbopack finds the source files first. **The fix for Docker builds with monorepo `file:` dependencies:** ```bash # Copy BOTH src and dist from the monorepo to the Docker context cp -r ../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections/src/* packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/src/ cp -r ../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections/dist/* packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/dist/ ``` The `packages/@ai-whisperers/` directory in the site repo is the Docker build context for `COPY . .` — Turbopack resolves from the `src/` subdirectory during `next build`. If only `dist/` is fresh but `src/` is stale, Turbopack compiles the stale source. **Diagnosis:** Check the builder-stage container: ```bash docker run --rm --target=builder grep 'newFieldName' /app/packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/src/component.tsx ``` If the new field is in `node_modules/` dist but NOT in `packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/src/`, the output SSG won't have it. ### Edge Cache Bypass for Verification Cloudflare edge cache can serve stale HTML for hours after deployment. Three bypass methods: **1. Direct container check (when you have Docker access):** ```bash docker exec $(docker ps -q -f name=) grep 'expected content' /app/.next/server/app/en/page.html ``` **2. `--resolve` bypass (bypasses DNS → Traefik directly):** ```bash curl -s --resolve "site.com:443:127.0.0.1" -H "Host: site.com" "https://site.com/en/page" ``` **3. Cache-busting headers (less reliable with aggressive CDN):** ```bash curl -sH "Cache-Control: no-cache" -H "Pragma: no-cache" "https://site.com/en/page" ``` **Note:** React renders text nodes with `` comment separators. `◷09:00` may render as `◷ 09:00` — simple `grep -oP '◷[^<]*'` may miss the text because of the intervening comment. Use `grep -oP '◷.*?09:00'` or check the full `

` element. ### Monorepo Package Components: Changes Must Go Upstream When the content you're adding comes from a component defined in a shared monorepo package (e.g. `@ai-whisperers/sections`), adding data to the JSON is only half the work. The component must also be updated to render the new field. **Workflow for adding a new rendered field to a monorepo-powered site:** 1. **Check where the component lives** — if it's imported from `@scope/sections`, the source is in the monorepo, not in the project's own source tree 2. **Find the source file** — navigate to `node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections/src/.tsx` (the `src/` is what Next.js compiles via `transpilePackages`) 3. **Edit the component** to read the new field from its `data` or `pageContent` prop 4. **Update the `dist/`** — if the component is consumed via `dist/` (look for `exports` in package.json), run the monorepo build or copy the modified source to dist 5. **Commit changes upstream** — the same source file lives in both `node_modules/` (symlinked) and the monorepo root (e.g. `/root/ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections/src/`). After testing, copy the change to the monorepo and commit it so it persists across `npm install` 6. **Force a full clean build** — `rm -rf .next && docker build --no-cache ...` because SSG snapshots may not pick up component changes on partial rebuilds 7. **Verify via browser** — navigate to the page and check the DOM for the new text **Diagnosis: "Data is in JSON but page doesn't show it"** - Check `__NEXT_DATA__` in page source — does `content: {}` or `content: {...}`? If empty, JSON failed to load - Check `dist/.js` for the new field name — if it's not in the compiled output, the `dist/` wasn't rebuilt - Check if the component returns early: `if (!d.title && !paragraphs.length) return null` — if the component only checks `title` and `paragraphs` but the new field lives under a different name, the data IS loaded but never reaches a rendering code path - Place the new section data INSIDE a known-rendered field structure when possible, or extend the component to add a new rendering branch Use `execute_code` with Python's `json` module for large JSON files: **CRITICAL: JSON field additions may not render.** Adding a new key like `resultsParagraphs` to the content JSON does NOT mean the page will display it. The page's React component must explicitly read and render that field. Before adding novel data that doesn't have an existing structural precedent in the file, check whether the page layout component actually consumes it — otherwise the data sits in the JSON unseen. To verify whether a JSON field renders on the page: 1. Deploy the changes (build + deploy) 2. Navigate to the page in a browser 3. Check the DOM for the expected text strings 4. If the text doesn't appear, the component doesn't render that key — either extend the component or restructure the data to use a known-rendered field ```python import json locales = ['es', 'en', 'nl', 'de'] for loc in locales: path = f'/root/nexa-paraguay/content/{loc}.json' with open(path) as f: data = json.load(f) # Check structure (en.json is flat, others are nested) if 'home' in data: home = data['home'] # ... make changes ... else: home = data # flat structure with open(path, 'w') as f: json.dump(data, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False) ``` **Critical:** Verify every locale file after changes: ```bash for f in content/*.json; do python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('$f'))" && echo "$f: OK"; done ``` ## Key Session Insight: Family vs Role Mapping When the founder is married to a Dutch national and the "client" WhatsApp contact is their daughter, **don't assume the daughter is the founder**. Common pattern in LATAM family businesses: | Role | Person | |------|--------| | Founder / CEO | Mother — lived abroad, has the experience, makes decisions | | Content & Tech contact | Daughter — passes feedback, handles day-to-day | | Technical partner | Son/daughter-in-law — builds the site | **The founder's own words are the source of truth for the business.** A daughter's interpretation is second-hand. Always ask: "who said this — the founder directly, or someone relaying?" When the founder sends WhatsApp audios, transcribe them verbatim and use them as the definitive positioning. Example: "acompañamiento de cerca" (close accompaniment) emerged from one audio and became the core tagline. ## Phase 5: PPR Content Verification 1. `rm -rf .next && npx next build` — must pass with all routes intact 2. Check route count didn't drop unexpectedly 3. Check PPR status on all major routes 4. Commit with a structured message listing what changed per category ## Phase 6: Update Roadmap + Client Analysis After implementing, update: - The upgrade tracker (`upgrade-tracker.json`) — change Todo→Done for completed items - The complete roadmap (`docs/13-upgrades/complete-roadmap.md`) — mark statuses - The client analysis (`docs/client-analysis-complete.md`) — refresh with new understanding ## Phase 7: Screenshot Baseline After all changes are deployed, create a screenshot baseline of every live page to document the current state: 1. **Identify all page slugs** from `nexa-pages/*.json` or equivalent 2. **Identify all blog article slugs** from content/blog/posts-*.json 3. **Capture full-page screenshots** for every slug × every locale using Playwright: ```bash python3 -c " import subprocess, os, json outdir = '/path/to/screenshots/full' os.makedirs(outdir, exist_ok=True) for slug in ['home', 'servicios', 'sobre', ...]: for loc in ['nl', 'en', 'es', 'de']: url = f'https://site.com/{loc}/{slug}' if slug != 'home' else f'https://site.com/{loc}' path = f'{outdir}/{loc}-{slug}.png' subprocess.run(['playwright', 'screenshot', '--viewport-size', '1440,900', '--full-page', url, path], timeout=60) " ``` 4. **Remove `screenshots/` from `.gitignore`** so the baseline is versioned 5. **Commit** with a message listing the scope (how many pages × locales) 6. **Later screenshots** can be compared against this baseline to show progress **Screenshot naming convention:** `{locale}-{slug}.png` — lowercase, no trailing slash. Blog articles: `{locale}-blog-{slug[:45]}.png`. ## Phase 1b: Pricing Model Discovery When market research assumptions about pricing turn out wrong after the client meeting, follow this pattern: ### The Problem A common pattern in professional-services client work: 1. You do market research → find competitors at $2,900-$6,900 2. You assume similar pricing and build everything around those tiers 3. Client meeting confirms price is **$1,500** — completely different The risk is that old assumptions linger in docs and mislead everyone. ### The Fix: Deprecation + Changelog + Restructure #### 1. Create a CHANGELOG.md Document every assumption that was invalidated. Table format: | Old Assumption | What You Had | New Reality | Source | |----------------|-------------|-------------|--------| | Core price | $2,900 | **$1,500** | Meeting §1.1 | | Revenue model | Fees = main income | **Commissions** = real money | Meeting §1.3 | | Costs/mo | $10K-$18K | **~$0 fixed** | Meeting §1.4 | Add a `CHANGELOG.md` to `docs/` with full before/after for every changed assumption. #### 2. Add Deprecation Banners For every doc that was built on wrong assumptions, add a banner at the top: ```markdown > **⚠️⚠️⚠️ DEPRECATED — NOT CURRENT ⚠️⚠️⚠️** > This document was built on the $X assumptions that were **invalidated** by the YYYY-MM-DD meeting. > See `docs/meetings/meeting-report-YYYY-MM-DD.md` and `docs/CHANGELOG.md` for current data. > Keep this file as historical reference only. DO NOT use for business decisions. ``` Files that typically need banners: - `pricing-matrix-complete.md` (if pricing changed) - `financial-model.md` (if revenue/cost projections changed) - `site-audit.md` (if client rejected recommendations) #### 3. Rethink the Pricing Model on Site The pattern that works for professional-services websites: ``` 1 base price (transparent, fixed) + day rates + commission-funded (free to client) + custom quotes ``` Not 4 program tiers. The client's real revenue model is different from what consultants assume. #### 4. Update Cross-References Point README.md, SOURCE_OF_TRUTH.md, and document hierarchy trees to the new meeting report, not the old pricing docs. --- ## Phase 1e: Client Discovery Meeting Prep (Spanish-Language Client) When you need to go through questions with a Spanish-speaking client in a structured meeting after research, the `meeting-prep` skill covers internal calendar briefs. For **client-facing meetings**, use this format. ### Structure a Walkable Document Organize questions into blocks ordered for conversation flow, not by topic importance. The first block should always be "show what we built" so the client sees progress. | Block | Topic | Questions | Time | |-------|-------|-----------|------| | 0 | Check-in + show & tell | 1 | 5 min | | 1 | **Pricing** (CRITICAL) | 8 | 12 min | | 2 | Services scope | 6 | 10 min | | 3 | Site content + voice | 6 | 10 min | | 4 | Photos & assets | 4 | 8 min | | 5 | WhatsApp bot + ops | 5 | 8 min | | 6 | Competition | 4 | 5 min | | 7 | Close + actions | — | 5 min | | **Total** | | **34-40** | **~60 min** | ### Each Question Format Every question needs: 1. **The exact wording** in the client's language (Spanish for Paraguayan clients). Not English with a translation thought — the actual Spanish question they'll hear. 2. **Context** — why we need it (1-2 sentences hidden from client, for our reference) 3. **Expected answer** — what they'll probably say (helps you prepare counter-questions) 4. **Action per answer** — what we do for each possible response. If answer X → do Y. If answer Z → do W. 5. **Notes field** — blank space to fill during the call ### Appendices - **Appendix A: Already Answered** — questions we DON'T ask again. List each with source meeting and confirmed answer. This prevents re-litigating settled topics. - **Appendix B: Error Table** — old assumptions vs new reality. Show this at the beginning when pricing discussion comes up so the client sees we understand the delta. ### Phase Communication Strategy Don't send all questions at once. Clients (especially small business owners) get overwhelmed. | Phase | What | Channel | |-------|------|---------| | **Urgent** (during meeting) | 3-4 pricing questions + photo request | In person / video call | | **This week** | Service definitions in a markable doc | Google Doc + WhatsApp | | **Build-time** | Operational questions per task | WhatsApp per item | ### Pitfalls - Don't use English wording with Spanish-speaking clients and expect them to follow. Write the question IN Spanish. - Don't re-ask settled questions. Appendix A exists for this reason — check it before the meeting. - If a client's family member relays information but the founder is the decision maker, the relayed info is NOT the source of truth. Always validate with the founder. - Build-time questions should be asked INDIVIDUALLY, not batched. The client will ignore a list of 10 WhatsApp messages but will answer a single question. - The "notes field" is critical. You will NOT remember what they said 30 minutes into the conversation. ## Phase 1f: Docs Restructure After Major Client Feedback ## Phase 1d: Docs Restructure After Major Client Feedback When new client info invalidates old documents, follow this process: ### 1. Audit the Damage Find every file that references the now-wrong assumptions: - Search for wrong prices, wrong tiers, wrong features - Check cross-references between docs - Note stale dates ### 2. Restructure the Directory Clean up loose root-level files: - Move standalone files into numbered subdirectories (01-client/, 09-market-intelligence/, etc.) - Move HTML deliverables to `docs/deliverables/` - Create `docs/meetings/` for meeting records - Move raw transcripts to `docs/meetings/` ### 3. Fix the Navigation Update `docs/README.md`: - Fix any wrong paths (e.g., repo paths that changed) - Point to the meeting report as the key document, not outdated pricing docs - Add status columns (✅ Current, ⚠️ Deprecated, 🟡 Needs Update) to file tables - Add a "Start Here" section pointing to CHANGELOG.md ### 4. Update SOURCE_OF_TRUTH.md - Add a note at the top: "For meeting outcome, see docs/meetings/meeting-report-YYYY-MM-DD.md" - Replace any "UPCOMING" sections with "OUTCOME" sections - Update document hierarchy tree - Update integrity check / status tables ### 5. Commit Message Format ``` docs: full restructuring post-YYYY-MM-DD - Created CHANGELOG.md — every assumption that changed - Added deprecation banners to pricing-matrix-complete.md, financial-model.md - Moved N root-level files into proper subdirectories - Moved N HTML deliverables to docs/deliverables/ - Fixed README.md wrong paths + cross-references - Updated SOURCE_OF_TRUTH.md with outcome sections ``` --- ## Pitfalls ## Founder-Direct vs Relayed Feedback Not all client feedback is equal. Two distinct patterns: ### Pattern A: Relayed Feedback (Assistant relays founder's words) - **Source:** Luana, Kiki, Ivan — someone who ISN'T the founder - **Signal:** "she says", "my mom wants", "Sonia said to remove" - **Risk:** Interpretation drift — the relay may soften, amplify, or filter what the founder actually wants - **Handling:** Implement what's asked, but flag any claim that seems to conflict with the founder's known values. Save the relayed message verbatim. - **Example:** Luana said "remove intake wizard" — relayed clearly, no interpretation. Implement immediately. ### Pattern B: Founder-Direct Feedback (Founder streams raw content) - **Source:** Sonia herself — WhatsApp text, voice notes, or in-person - **Signal:** First-person narrative ("I lived 7 years in the Netherlands"), raw unstructured stream of consciousness - **Risk:** The content is gold but unstructured — needs extraction into a gap matrix - **Handling:** 1. Save the **verbatim message** to a reference file 2. **Do NOT** dump it directly into the site content — it's raw, it needs structuring 3. Extract specific data points: numbers ("10 personas"), services ("Airbnb temporal, chip local, colegios"), process steps 4. Cross-reference against the live site's content JSON files to build a gap matrix 5. The gap matrix shows what's already implemented vs what's missing 6. Propose structured content changes for approval before implementing ### The Gap Matrix Process (Founder-Direct Only) When a founder sends free-form narrative content: 1. **Read all current locale content files** (es/en/nl/de content/*.json) 2. **Build a matrix** with columns: `Feature | Has Content? | Where | Missing?` 3. For each distinct point in the founder's message: - Is it already in the site content? If yes, mark ✅ and note where - Is it partially covered? 🟡 - Is it completely absent? 🔴 — this is a gap 4. Present the matrix to the user before implementing 5. Only implement gaps after the user confirms **Example matrix output:** ``` | Feature | Status | Notes | |---|---|---| | 7 años en Holanda | 🔴 No está | Sobre Nexa dice genérico | | Música → mamá | 🔴 No está | Historia personal faltante | | 10 personas ayudadas | 🔴 No está | Primer stat real disponible | | Airbnb temporal/chip local | 🔴 No está | Proceso incompleto | | Colegios/seguro médico | 🔴 No está | No mencionado en servicios | | Contaduría network confiable | 🔴 No está | Honestidad = valor | | Requisitos de residencia (lista) | ✅ NL/EN | ES/DE faltan | ``` ## Multi-Tenant Path Cleanup (Post-Migration) After migrating a site from a multi-tenant builder (path pattern `/s/{locale}/{site}/...`) to standalone (`/{locale}/...`), EVERY content file may still contain broken multi-tenant paths in: - Footer column links - Header navigation CTAs - Hero section CTAs - Section-level CTA hrefs - Blog content (MDX/JSON inline links) - `ctaHref` on booking forms - `linkHref` on privacy/legal notices - Any `"/s/{locale}/{site}/..."` string in JSON **Verification grep:** ```bash grep -rn "/s/[a-z]/" content/*.json grep -rn '"/servicios"' content/*.json # missing locale prefix ``` **Fix scope:** Search ALL 4 locale files. The locale code in the path varies (`/s/nl/...`, `/s/en/...`, `/s/es/...`, `/s/de/...`). Replace with the corresponding `/{locale}/` prefix. ## Page Removal (Cross-Cutting) When a client says "remove page X", it's not just deleting a file. The page is referenced across the entire site structure: | Concern | Where to fix | What to change | |---------|--------------|----------------| | Route registration | `nexa-pages/{slug}.json` | Delete the file — Next.js won't generate the route | | Sitemap | `src/app/sitemap.ts` or similar | Remove from any fallback/static arrays | | Nav | `site.json` navigation array | Remove the nav entry | | Nav (per-locale) | `content/*.json` navigation items | Remove the nav entry in ALL 4 files | | Footer links | `content/*.json` footer columns | Remove in ALL 4 files | | Internal CTA references | `content/*.json` section CTAs | Replace `href: "/programas"` with a valid alternative page | | Types | `src/types.ts` | Remove `{slug}Page?: PageSectionContent` from SiteContent | | Booking forms | `src/components/BookingFormSection.tsx` | Update "back to X" links | | Page content blocks | `content/*.json` | Remove the entire `{slug}Page` block if it exists | | Empty placeholder strings | `content/*.json` (en/nl specifically) | Remove 70+ `"{slug}Page": ""` entries from nested section objects | **Verification:** After removal, grep for the slug name across the entire repo — any remaining reference is a broken link waiting to happen. ## Locale Leak Detection Spanish text in NL and EN locale files is a common artifact of template-based content generation. Check specifically: - **"En resumen"** heading — appears in `highlights.title` under `whyCountryPage` or similar sections - **FAQ questions in Spanish** — `"¿Cuántos viajes..."`, `"¿Cuánto tiempo..."` - **Highlight card titles** — `"8–12 semanas"`, `"Un solo interlocutor"`, `"Precio fijo"` - **Highlight descriptions** — `"Proceso completo desde presentación."`, `"Nexa coordina todos los profesionales."` **Fix:** Translate each to the target locale. For EN: `"8–12 weeks"`, `"Single point of contact"`, `"Fixed price"`. For NL: `"8–12 weken"`, `"Eén aanspreekpunt"`, `"Vaste prijs"`. **Search pattern across all locales:** ```bash grep -rn '"¿' content/nl.json content/en.json content/de.json # Spanish question marks grep -rn '"En resumen"' content/ # Spanish summary heading ``` - **Client action blockers (photo, QR scan, DNS).** Three common items that I CANNOT implement because they require client-side action: 1. **Photos** — Hero images, team portraits, location shots. The site uses AI placeholders. Nothing to replace until the client sends real photos. 2. **WhatsApp bot QR** — The bot is configured and running. The QR must be scanned from the client's WhatsApp app (Settings → Linked Devices). This is a 30-second physical action that unlocks lead qualification. 3. **Domain DNS** — The server (Traefik) is configured to listen for the custom domain. The client must create an A record at their registrar pointing to the VPS IP. Document all three with clear next-step instructions when the session ends. They are NOT technical blockers. - **PPR static shell caching** — When PPR (`cacheComponents: true`) is active, the static HTML shell for `[locale]` is frozen at build time. Changing `nexa-pages/home.json` does NOT update the visible content until the Docker image is rebuilt AND `--force` deployed. `docker service update --image ...` with the same tag may skip the restart. Always use `docker service update --force --image ...`. - **SectionRenderer content key mismatch** — Components like `HeroSection` do `pageContent.hero` assuming `pageContent` is the `home` sub-object. If the section config has `"content": "home.hero"`, the resolved data is `home.hero` but the component looks for `pageContent.hero` which is `home.hero.hero` = null. Change content key to `"home"` so `pageContent = content.home` and `pageContent.hero` resolves correctly. For sections that use `data` directly (FeedbackSection), use the deep path `"home.feedback"`. ", "file_type": ".md"}

{"success": true, "name": "client-feedback-pipeline", "file": "references/client-proposal-document-format.md", "content": "# Client Proposal Document Format

For presenting multiple options to a client (Sonia or any professional-services client) for them to choose and validate.

## When to Use

- After initial discovery/research, before building
- Client needs to see options, not just one recommendation
- Several unknowns remain that only the client can resolve
- Client has told us data that invalidated our assumptions, and we need to rebuild the plan

## Structure

### Header

```markdown
# [Client Name] — Propuesta Completa

> **Propósito:** [one-sentence purpose]
> **Documento vivo** — lo actualizamos después de cada reunión.
> **Formato:** Opciones con su contexto, costo, esfuerzo y recomendación.
```

### Each Proposal Section

Every decision point uses this structure:

```markdown
### X.X — [Decision Name]

**Contexto:** [Why this decision matters. 2-3 sentences.]

| Opción | Descripción | Ejemplo |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| **A** | Option A description | Concrete example |
| **B** | Option B description | Concrete example |
| **C** | Option C description | Concrete example |

**Recomendación:** ✅ Which option and why.

**Sonia decide:** ☐ A ☐ B ☐ C
```

### Additional Metadata Per Section (optional, when context is complex)

```
| Campo | Qué significa |
|-------|--------------|
| **Esfuerzo** | Hours to implement |
| **Impacto** | Bajo / Medio / Alto |
| **Riesgo** | What could go wrong |
| **Dependencia** | What we need from client to start |
```

### Document Sections (Typical Order)

1. **Pricing** — base price, tiers/add-ons, commissions, kids, quotes
2. **Site Structure** — pages to keep/delete/create, navigation, lead magnets
3. **Content** — voice, slogan, personal story borrador
4. **New Features** — calculators, maps, bots
5. **Competitor Analysis** — what to steal, adapt, avoid
6. **Photos & Assets** — checklist of what we need from client, with urgency
7. **Action Plan** — 3 phases: immediate (no dependency), post-meeting, ongoing
8. **Meeting Checklist** — questions to resolve in the meeting, sorted 🔴🟡🟢
9. **Client's Ideas** — blank space for them to add their own
10. **Approval** — signature block with date

### Language

- Always write in the **client's language** (Spanish for Paraguayan clients)
- Use "vos" form for Sonia (Paraguayan Spanish)
- Each question must have the EXACT wording the client will hear
- Expected answers help prepare counter-questions
- Notes fields are critical — you will NOT remember what they said 30 min into the conversation

### Key Behaviors

- Don't present only one option — clients want to choose
- Don't ask the same question twice — Appendix A exists for already-answered questions
- Don't send the full document as a cold message — walk through it together in a meeting
- After the meeting, the client marks what they approved with checkboxes
- Update the document after the meeting with their decisions
- Commit to the repo as the new source of truth
", "file_type": ".md"}

{"success": true, "name": "client-feedback-pipeline", "file": "references/competitor-analysis-workflow.md", "content": "# Deep Competitor Analysis Workflow

For researching a competitor's full site structure, extracting pricing, product strategy, content approach, and producing actionable recommendations.

## When to Use

- Client names a specific competitor they're worried about
- You discover a strong competitor during market research
- Need to understand WHY a competitor is successful before positioning against them
- Client wants to borrow (not copy) elements from another site

## Step 1: Full Site Reconnaissance

Use `browser_navigate` to visit every major page of the competitor's site:

| Page Type | What to Extract |
|-----------|----------------|
| Home | Slogan, hero message, main CTA, featured content |
| Services/Products | All offerings with prices, what's included per tier |
| Pricing | Visible prices, payment options, discounts, currency |
| About/Team | Who are the key people, their backgrounds, credibility |
| FAQ | Objections they anticipate, their answers, tone |
| Blog/Content | Post frequency, topics, content mix (% politics, % practical, % community) |
| Contact | Lead capture mechanism, WhatsApp vs form vs email |
| Lead magnets | Ebooks, checklists, calculators — what captures emails |
| Expeditions/Events | Tours, packages, itineraries, prices |
| Legal pages | Compliance, delivery policy, refund policy |

## Step 2: Deep Page Content Extraction

For key pages (pricing, services, expeditions), extract the FULL content with `web_extract` or `web_extract_plus`. Don't rely on the snapshot alone — snapshots skip detailed text blocks.

Look for:
- **Exact prices** (including currency)
- **What's included vs what's extra**
- **Testimonial content** (who they quote, what clients say)
- **FAQ structure** (how they frame objections)
- **CTA phrasing** (urgency, risk-reversal, guarantees)

## Step 3: Content Strategy Analysis

Categorize the competitor's blog/content into buckets:

| Category | % of Posts | Example Topics |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| Tax/legal updates | ~30% | Regulatory changes, exit taxes |
| Political/freedom content | ~25% | Ideological topics, controversial figures |
| Community events | ~20% | Group tours, gatherings, aftermovies |
| Practical advice | ~15% | How-to guides, requirements, costs |
| Personal stories | ~10% | Founder experiences, client testimonials |

## Step 4: Build the Comparison Table

| Dimension | Competitor | Our Client |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Language | Dutch only | Spanish + 4 locales |
| Price | €2,800 (~$3,100) | **$1,500** |
| Target | Freedom-seekers, investors | Families, retirees |
| Tone | Political, fear-based | Personal, warm, "relax" |
| Location | Rural (Hohenau) | Urban (Asunción) |
| Property focus | Dedicated agent (Century 21) | Commission-based (free to client) |
| Group tours | ✅ Expeditions (€3,397-€5,397) | ❌ |
| Post-residency | Limited | ✅ Strong ($200/day, up to 1 year) |
| Blog | Active, political + tax | 30 posts (generic AI, needs overhaul) |
| Lead magnets | ✅ Multiple ebooks | ❌ Not yet |
| Bot | Telegram group (manual) | ✅ WhatsApp bot (coming) |
| Location data | None | ✅ 1,485 places with GPS |
| Overhead | High (expeditions, marketing) | Near zero |

## Step 5: Identify What to STEAL

Things to copy directly (they're generic best practices):

| Tactic | How to Adapt |
|--------|-------------|
| FAQ page | Compile client's answered questions into one page |
| Email capture on every page | Footer + blog + lead magnets + 404 page |
| Lead magnets | "10 cosas que nadie te contó sobre Paraguay" |
| Testimonials slider | Rotating testimonials on services page |
| Transparent pricing | Show price directly |
| 404 page with capture | Redirect errors to ebook download |

## Step 6: Identify What to ADAPT

Things to take and modify for the client's positioning:

| Competitor's Version | Client's Version |
|---------------------|-----------------|
| "Plan B" (fear/escape) | "Un nuevo capítulo" (positive/aspirational) |
| Legalistic blog post | Simple, personal, visual timeline |
| Residency timing calculator | Tax calculator |
| "Why Paraguay" (freedom) | "Why Paraguay" (quality of life, family) |

## Step 7: Identify What to AVOID

Things that DON'T fit the client's brand:

| Don't Copy | Why |
|-----------|-----|
| Political/fear-based messaging | Client is apolitical |
| Paid group expeditions | Too complex for client's bandwidth |
| YouTube channel | Client doesn't want to be a content creator |
| Crypto payments | Not needed at this volume |
| Container shipping | Client's clients come with luggage, not households |
| Telegram group | WhatsApp is the client's platform |

## Step 8: Identify the Client's ADVANTAGES

Things the competitor CANNOT easily copy:

| Advantage | Why It Matters |
|-----------|---------------|
| Price (47% cheaper) | Massive competitive gap |
| Personal story + reinvention | More relatable than political commentary |
| Family focus | Different target segment |
| Post-residency support | Competitor offers one-time service |
| Commission-free property help | "Free to client, paid by seller" |
| Neighborhood intelligence | 1,485 GPS places — no competitor has this |
| Local (not expat) | Understands both worlds |
| Lower overhead | Near zero costs = can underprice |

## Output Document Structure

```markdown
# [Competitor Name] — Competitive Analysis & Learnings

> **URL:** https://...
> **Competitor to:** [Client Name]

## 1. WHO THEY ARE
## 2. PRICING & PRODUCT STRUCTURE
## 3. SITE STRUCTURE & PAGES
## 4. CONTENT STRATEGY
## 5. WHAT WE CAN STEAL (Ethically)
## 6. WHAT TO ADAPT
## 7. WHAT NOT TO COPY
## 8. OUR ADVANTAGES
## 9. RECOMMENDED ACTIONS (Steal / Adapt / Avoid)
## 10. DIRECT COMPARISON TABLE
```
", "file_type": ".md"}
Both references are in place. The skill now has two new reference files. Let me verify the full listing.
